title: u.r.trax and immersive music for the mind
author: Allastair Voss
body:
[spotify:artist/5bGQtNvk4LrSUTZGzBiZq5]

Ines Boullant grew up a drummer. By her early teens she was producing on whatever software she could borrow. By 2019 she had picked an alias, u.r.trax, short for under rave, and bought her first controller. Six years later she sits comfortably among the most interesting techno producers working out of Paris, with releases on Hector Oaks's KAOS, Nina Kraviz's trip and Ellen Allien's BPitch.

Her music is hard to file. Folge Mir, the 2020 KAOS debut, leaned into trippy, weightless techno. Moral Krisis, on the sub-label KAOS-URTRAX, was bonier and meaner. Atom Heart on trip slipped sideways into psychedelia. Br0ken on BPitch sounded almost like a pop song wearing armour. Through all of it she has kept the same description in her bio: immersive music for the mind. It is the right phrase. The records do not want to entertain you. They want to relocate you.

## What Immersive Actually Means

Most producers who claim immersion are describing density. More elements, more layers, more sonic information competing for attention. Boullant works differently. Her tracks create immersion through space and tension, through the deliberate removal of resolution at moments where other producers would offer a drop or a hook. You are suspended inside the music rather than being pushed through it.

This technique is rooted in her background as a drummer. She understands rhythm as something that can carry meaning through what it withholds as much as through what it delivers. The kick patterns in her sets are not simple timekeeping. They breathe. They hesitate. They drag against the grid in ways that create physical unease and then resolve it, or don't.

Listen to Moral Krisis and pay attention to the hi-hat patterns. They shift in ways that are not random but are not metrically obvious either. The effect is subtle disorientation, a sense that the ground beneath the track is not fully stable. That instability is the point. It keeps the listener in a state of active attention rather than passive reception.

Atom Heart, the trip release, extends this approach into explicitly psychedelic territory. The synth textures are slower to develop, the transitions more liquid, the track more concerned with atmosphere than propulsion. It is the clearest demonstration that Boullant can apply her rhythmic intelligence to music that is not primarily about a dancefloor. The track works in headphones with the same conviction it works at volume.

## The Label Trajectory

The three labels she has released on tell their own story. KAOS is Hector Oaks's vehicle, Berlin-adjacent, technically rigorous, committed to a slow-burn version of techno that rewards repeated listening rather than immediate impact. Trip is Nina Kraviz's imprint, which leans into psychedelia and the outer edges of club music. BPitch is Ellen Allien's label, founded in 2000, one of the institutions of Berlin electronic music.

Each release has moved her into a different listening community while maintaining the coherence of the u.r.trax sound. She is not chasing each label's audience by making music that sounds like what that label typically releases. She is bringing her own approach and letting it find its place. The fact that it has found a place on all three says something about the quality of the work.

These are not labels that release music out of politeness. Oaks, Kraviz, and Allien are three of the more exacting figures in European techno, all of whom have built their own sounds over decades and are protective of the context their labels create. For Boullant to be welcomed across all three, from different aesthetic directions, is a specific kind of credential.

## Live as the Core Practice

Live she is closer to a sound designer than a selector. Six-hour sets at Bassiani in Tbilisi, last winter, moved through electro, raw techno, dark trance and the kind of backroom psychedelia that only works at four in the morning. She talks about DJing as an improvised dialogue between sound, space and bodies. You can hear her listening for that dialogue in real time. The mixes are not rehearsed. The transitions feel earned.

Bassiani is worth naming specifically because it represents a particular standard. The Tbilisi club, built inside a former Soviet sports complex, operates with sound system specifications and audience seriousness that most European clubs cannot match. Artists who play there are expected to commit to the full arc of the night. That Boullant is booking there regularly, for extended durations, confirms the reputation she has built in the circuit that cares most about this.

## The Paris Context and the Refusal to Specialise

What sets her apart from the broader Paris scene is the refusal to specialise. She is not chasing one peak-time sound. She is building a vocabulary that travels between fun, beauty and chaos without losing its centre. That patience is rare in club music, where most careers reward a single recognisable trick.

Paris has produced electronic music of genuine ambition across several generations, from the house and techno underground of the 1990s through to the contemporary generation working across deconstructed club, industrial techno, and hardware-oriented production. Boullant fits none of these categories cleanly, which is the correct place to be. Her closest predecessors, if any name must be reached for, are in the tradition of artists who treat the DJ set as research, who document findings in studio releases, and who refuse to let either practice constrain the other.

Her own imprint, Ultra Rare, is a further expression of this independence. Running a label, even a small one, changes the relationship to the industry in ways that matter. You control what gets released, how it is framed, when it arrives. You answer to no one's aesthetic vision but your own. That is a structural choice as much as an artistic one, and Boullant has made it early.

## What Is Coming

New material is due this year through Ultra Rare and a new collaboration on a label she has not yet announced. SHAPE+ has her on its 2025 platform of artists to watch. Rinse FM gives her a regular show. She is, very visibly, the next chapter of European techno.

Start with Br0ken. Then sit with Atom Heart in headphones. Then book the next ticket to a four-deck u.r.trax set you can find.
